Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent routine, honestly. I'd block out about three hours each day after my extracurriculars to sit down with my courses. I'm the type who needs to physically do things to learn, so I'd write out notes by hand, work through practice problems, that kind of stuff. The routine felt comfortable even if the material didn't always stick the way I wanted it to.
Q2: I really tried to stick to regular times because that's just how I function best. Sundays were always my big study days, and I'd do smaller chunks during the week. Deadlines would sometimes throw me off, but I'd try to get back to my schedule as soon as possible. Having that structure kept me from feeling too overwhelmed, even when things got stressful.
Q3: Usually it was just part of my daily checklist—log in, see what's new, check announcements. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline or a new video posted. It became kind of automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. I wasn't always super engaged when I logged in, but I showed up.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it just became habit. I'd finish dinner, open my laptop, and the LMS was one of the first tabs I'd check. The mindfulness exercises they introduced actually helped me settle into that routine better—I'd do a quick breathing thing and then start studying. It wasn't exciting, but it was consistent.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful for me because I could pause them and actually try things out as I watched. I spent a lot of time on those—like over four hours total. The discussion boards were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that clicked better for me. Anything I could interact with physically or practically felt more valuable.
Q6: I'll be honest, I kind of ignored the reading materials a lot. They were just walls of text and my brain would wander. I'd skim them but not really absorb much. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because they felt like busywork when I was already spending so much time on other stuff. If it didn't feel hands-on, I probably didn't prioritize it.
Q7: If I could actually do something with the information—apply it, practice it, test myself—then it felt worth my time. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so just reading or listening passively makes me bored really fast. Activities that had clear outcomes or that I could physically work through kept me engaged. Otherwise I'd zone out even if I was technically doing the work.
Q8: Required activities felt like boxes to check, which I'd do because that's just who I am. Optional stuff was different—I'd only do it if it seemed genuinely helpful or interesting. Most of the time optional things felt like extras I couldn't afford to spend energy on, especially with four online courses going. I had to be strategic about where I put my effort.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. I'd download the lecture slides and print them out so I could write all over them. I also used YouTube a lot for supplementary explanations and practiced problems from textbooks I borrowed from the library. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical materials spread out on my desk, not on the screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then spend an hour afterward working through related problems in a notebook. Or I'd take the concepts and try to explain them out loud to myself while pacing around my room. None of that shows up anywhere, but that's where the real learning happened for me.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download PDFs or save videos to watch offline because my internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't afford to waste data rewatching things. Once I had the materials, I'd work with them multiple times without ever logging back in. The LMS was more like a pickup point than where I actually studied.
Q12: Around midterms everything got harder. I had assignments piling up across all four courses and my extracurriculars were demanding more time. I felt bored with the material too—like I was just going through motions without really caring. The mindfulness stuff helped a bit with the stress, but the boredom was harder to shake.
Q13: For sure. When I was tired or had a lot going on, I'd still log in because that's my routine, but I wasn't really present. I'd watch videos at 1.5x speed just to get through them, or skim discussion posts without really engaging. The workload from four courses meant I was always triaging what got my real attention.
Q14: Yeah, this happened more than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I was physically there doing the motions but not actually processing anything. It looked like engagement but it was really just me being present without being engaged.
Q15: The assignments were the big thing I kept putting off. I'd do all the other stuff—watch videos, check discussions—but when it came to actually completing and submitting work, I'd procrastinate. I think part of it was fear of not doing well, and part was just that assignments felt overwhelming compared to passive activities.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd know something was due and still wait until close to the deadline to really focus on it. My routine kept me logging in regularly, but the deadlines determined when I'd actually produce anything. Without them, I probably would have just kept consuming content forever.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback honestly, which was discouraging. When I did get comments on discussion posts or assignments, it motivated me to try harder next time. But the feedback was sparse and sometimes came too late to really help me improve for the next thing. More consistent feedback would have kept me more engaged.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard—watch this, read that, discuss, submit. It worked okay for keeping me on track with my routine, but it didn't really excite me. I would have liked more hands-on projects or interactive elements. The structure felt designed for people who learn by reading, not by doing.
Q19: The way everything was broken into weekly modules made it easy to follow my routine, which I appreciated. But the heavy reliance on text-based content made it harder for me to stay engaged. If there had been more simulations or practical exercises built into the LMS itself, I think I would have participated more actively.
Q20: I think that's me, honestly. I logged in a lot, watched tons of videos, participated in discussions, but my assignment completion was terrible. For me it was about the gap between consuming content and producing work. Engaging with materials felt manageable; creating something to submit felt like a bigger hurdle that I kept avoiding.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone downloads everything and studies offline, or if they're just naturally good at the material, they might not need to be on the LMS much. I spent a lot of time studying outside the system, but it didn't translate to good exam scores for me. The LMS activity and actual learning aren't always connected.
Q22: Logs can show when you showed up and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually thinking or just going through motions. They miss all the offline studying, the physical note-taking, the conversations with classmates outside the platform. They capture presence but not depth of engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or checking boxes—it's being curious, asking questions, wanting to master the material. I had the routine down but I was often bored, so my engagement was more surface-level than I wanted it to be.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with boredom even while maintaining my routine. It wouldn't capture all the offline studying I did with printed notes and textbooks. It also wouldn't show that despite my low assignment completion, I was genuinely trying to learn—I just couldn't translate that into submitted work. My effort was real even if my results weren't.
